text: The surgeon uses a sponge to retract the small bowel, revealing the tattoo-marked area of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon uses a sponge to retract the small bowel, revealing the tattooed area of the sigmoid colon, which is marked for identification.
The surgeon continues to adjust the elevation of the sigmoid colon, with assistance visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to adjust the elevation of the sigmoid colon, with assistance visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the tissue over the sacral promontory using a vessel sealer, opting for this instrument instead of scissors.
The surgeon opens over the sacral promontory using a vessel sealer, opting for this instrument instead of scissors for the dissection.
The surgeon uses an assist to hold the small bowel aside, maintaining a clear surgical field for the procedure.
The surgical team uses an assist to keep the small bowel out of the way, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg and left side up, as indicated by the altimeter and numerical display.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon without the use of ureteral catheters, indicating a careful approach to avoid ureteral injury.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection of the colon without the use of ureteral catheters, indicating a careful approach to avoid ureteral injury.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues using surgical instruments.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues using surgical instruments.
The surgeon clears lymphatic fat around the vessel, which is starting to come into view, revealing the underlying anatomical structure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to clear lymphatic fat around the vessel, with the structure starting to come into view. For finer dissection, scissors are chosen to precisely separate tissue.
The surgeon uses scissors for fine dissection around the inferior mesenteric vessels, preferring them over the vessel sealer for precise tissue separation.
The surgeon focuses on the vessel, performing a high ligation using the vessel sealer, with the vessel clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the vessel, performing a high ligation using a vessel sealer, with the vessel clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) close to the dissection site and decides to ligate it at the current level due to the patient's adiposity.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) close to the dissection area and decides to ligate it at the current level due to the patient's adiposity.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, using a sponge to protect the ureter while progressing laterally.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, using a sponge to protect the ureter while progressing laterally.
The surgeon visualizes the sponge through the peritoneum laterally and creates a window to connect the dissection from the upper to the lower section.
The surgeon visualizes the sponge through the peritoneum on the lateral side and creates a window to connect the dissection from the superior to inferior aspect.
The surgeon completes the high ligation of the mesentery vessels and proceeds with the lateral dissection, ensuring adequate reach for the anastomosis without mobilizing the spinal flexor.
The surgeon completes the high ligation of the mesentery vessels, preparing for the anastomosis without fully mobilizing the spinal flexor during the lateral dissection.
The surgeon visually searches for the ureter in the surgical field, ensuring its identification and protection during the procedure.
The surgeon visually searches for the ureter within the surgical field, ensuring its identification and protection during the procedure.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect and take the proximal mesentery, starting from just underneath the inferior mesenteric vessel, using careful technique to separate the mesentery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect and take the proximal mesentery from just underneath the inferior mesenteric vessel, with the cut edge of the mesentery visibly exposed.
The surgeon displays the cut edge of the tissue, highlighting the area where dissection has been completed.

The surgeon repositions the surgical field to identify the proximal margin, aiming for a distance of at least six centimeters from the tumor. The final margin is established at approximately ten centimeters proximal to the tumor.
The surgeon repositions to identify the proximal margin, aiming for a distance of at least six centimeters from the tumor. The final proximal margin is approximately ten centimeters from the tumor.
The surgeon cleans the valve wall to prepare for a more secure application of the stapler.
The surgeon cleans the valve wall to prepare for secure stapling, followed by cleaning the distal margin of the colon.
The surgeon cleans the distal margin at the recto sigmoid junction, where the teniae coli visibly splay out.
The surgeon focuses on the recto sigmoid junction, where the teniae coli visibly splay out, indicating the anatomical transition between the rectum and sigmoid colon.
The surgeon checks tissue perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence, with the proximal stapler in place to ensure adequate blood flow to the resection margins.
The surgeon checks tissue perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) dye, with the proximal stapler in place to ensure adequate blood flow to the resected bowel segment.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the distal margin of the colon, preparing to staple the tissue at the recto sigmoid junction.
The surgeon applies a stapler to the distal section of the colon, securing the tissue in preparation for resection.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis using a sewn technique after the specimen has been separated, joining the bowel ends within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis by sewing the two staple lines together using a 3-0 nine-inch V-Loc suture, following the separation of the specimen.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 nine-inch B-lock suture to join the two staple lines, carefully aligning the edges of the bowel to ensure the enterotomy, colotomy, and proctotomy are even on both the proximal and distal sides.
The surgeon aligns the edges of the bowel, ensuring that the enterotomy and colotomy are even on both the proximal and distal sides, preparing for a secure anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a proctotomy, visibly clearing rectal fat from the lateral aspect on the near side of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a proctotomy, visibly clearing rectal fat from the lateral aspect on the near side of the rectum.

The surgeon performs a colotomy, creating two wide openings in the bowel. Using a 3-0 nine-inch relock suture, the inner layer is sewn starting from the middle and extending laterally around the left side. Indocyanine green (ICG) is applied, visually highlighting the colonic side before the rectal side, with both areas lighting up distinctly.

With the rectal fat cleared, the surgeon creates a colotomy with two wide openings and begins sewing the inner layer using a 3-0 nine-inch relock suture, starting in the middle and moving laterally around the left side. The surgeon checks perfusion using ICG, observing that the colonic side lights up before the rectal side, but both illuminate clearly.
The surgeon places each stitch precisely during the intracorporeal sewn anastomosis, maneuvering around the far corner of the bowel.
The surgeon places each stitch precisely during the sewn anastomosis, maneuvering around the far corner of the bowel to ensure accurate alignment and secure connection.
The surgeon transitions to suturing the canal, ensuring visibility of the near side by carefully placing the second and third VLAC sutures, starting in the middle and sewing around the near corner.
The surgeon transitions to a canal approach, ensuring visibility of the near side by placing the second and third VLAC sutures. The sutures are started in the middle and sewn around the near corner.

The surgeon navigates around the near corner of the surgical field, using reticulated instruments to switch to the canal. The backhand suturing technique is employed to secure the stitch with precision.
The surgeon navigates around the near corner, switching to the canal using reticulated instruments. The surgeon backhands the suturing with precision, securing it back to the previous stitch.
The surgeon adjusts the far suture, bringing it back towards the midline to avoid tying knots in the corners during the anastomosis.
The surgeon adjusts the far suture, bringing it back towards the midline to avoid tying knots in the corners during the anastomosis.
The surgeon ties two sutures together and uses the original suture to invaginate the inner and outer staple lines anteriorly, securing them with a silk suture.
The surgeon ties two sutures together and uses the original suture to invaginate the inner and outer staple lines anteriorly, securing them to the silk suture.
The surgeon performs a leak test using a 19mm rigid scope, driving it through the widely patent anastomosis to visually inspect for any air leaks.
The surgeon performs a leak test using a 19mm rigid scope, inspecting the anastomosis for air leaks. The scope is driven through the widely patent anastomosis.
